moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the Zukertort Opening, a flexible first move that controls the critical d4 and e5 squares while keeping our options open—we don't commit to the pawn structure yet like we would with 1.e4 or 1.d4. The knight is perfectly placed on f3 to support a future center push, and it's already eyeing d4 and e5. This move was favored by 19th-century master Siegbert Tarrasch and modern hypermodern players who prefer controlling the center from a distance rather than occupying it immediately.

Black plays the Basman Defense, a quirky and rare response that immediately gains space on the kingside and prevents White from playing Ng5 (which can sometimes be annoying). However, h6 is considered slow and passive—it doesn't address the center or develop pieces, so Black is essentially wasting time with a prophylactic move. You should continue developing your pieces naturally (likely with d4, c4, or g3 depending on your setup) and build a space advantage in the center while Black hasn't committed to a clear plan.
